SAN FRANCISCO
wasH TNGToN, o.c
APR 2 i s92
ORANGE COUNTY
PTLLSBURY MADTSON 5. SUTRO FPPC
su rlE 335
ZT55 CAPITOL MALL
SACRAM ENTO. CALIFORN IA 9S8I'I
TELEPHONE (9r6) 44r-O666
iELECOPTER (9r6) 44r-35A3
\
April 21, 199 2
EAND DBLIVBRED
Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 958l-4
Re: Revolving Door Issues
Dear Scott:
This letter constitutes a request for formal written advice
pursuant to Government Code section 831-14(b) on behalf of The
GCA Group ("GCA"), a private consulting f irm. Our questions
concern the extent to which Ms. Margit Aramburu, an employee of
GCA, is subject to the "revolving door" provisions of the
PoLitical Reform Act of L974, as amended (the "Act" ) by virtue
of her previous service with the San Francisco Bay Conservation
Development Cormission ("BCDC"). We are requesting written
confirmation of telephone advice provided by John walLace on
April 14 and 16, 1992.
I. 
FACTUAT BACKGROI'ND.
For more than tweLve years, Ms. Aramburu $ras an employee of
BCDC, a state agency. During her l-ast five years with BCDC, Ms.
Aramburu's position r.vas Bay Design Analyst. Her duties included
the review of development plans for cornpleteness, comparing
plans to terms of exi-sting permits, processing permits
(including the analysis of permit applications), preparing staff
analyses of issues and presenting these analyses before the
BCDC, and preparing couunents on Environmental Impact Reports
("EIRS"). Ms. Arahburu left the BCDC on June 16, 1991,. She
currently is employed by GCA.
In November of 1991, the Chief of Permj-ts of BCDC asked Ms.
Aramburu to return to the BCDC to fill 
a part-time, 90-day
limited term position because of an office "emergency. "
11061401


Scott fla1labrin, Esq.
April 21, 1992
Page 2
Apparently, the staff engineer had just left the office and the
BCDC had not fiIled Ms. Araurburu's prior position. 
This meant
that there was no gtaff available to review development p1ans.
Ms. Aramburu agreed to assist the agency on a part-time,
temporary basis, until February -l , L992.
Her part-time work was limited to the review of plans
submitted for completeness and comparing them to the terms of
existing permits. Her duties did not require exercise of
discretion. 
Ms. Aramburu wae not asked to procesrs any permits,
prepare any staff analysis of IEEues for the BCDC, present any
recommendations to the BCDC, attend any staff meeting, prepare
any written conments on EIRs. or prepare analyses of any permit
applications. Atthough lts. Aramburu was classified as a Bay
Design Analyst, BCDC did not require her to file a Statement of
Economic Interests as a limited-term employee. In addition, the
exit form executed at the conclusion of her limited-term service
reaffirmed that her formal termination date from the BCDC was
June 15, 1991 .
Several years a!Jo, BCDC issued a permit to Pier 39, Inc.
for the Pier 39 project in San Francisco. During her tenure at
BCDC, Ms. Aramburu was not involved in the initial 
Pier 39
permit proceeding, although she did work on a since completed
amendment to the Pier 39 permj-t concerning the development of a
project know as "Underwater World." Ms. Aranburu also reviewed
plans for remodeJ.ing of various specific commercial facilities
at Pier 39 for compliance with the terms of the Pier 39 permit.
GCA would like to work with Pier 39, Inc. and its tenants
who submit plans to the BCDC for remodeling of specific
commercial sites located on Pier 39. Such remodel plans would
not be connected with any prior remodel plans reviewed by Ms.
Aramburu, although the tenant may occasionally be one whose
plans Ms. Aramburu reviewed during her service at BCDC. The
remodel" plans also would have no connection to the Underwater
World amendment to the Pier 39 permit.
In addition. GCA was recently selected as part of a three
firm team to perform planning work for the City of Val1ejo. The
City is studying possible recategorization of a parcel of City-
owned land from Port Priority Use Area to another or no
designation in the Bay Plan of the BCDC. Ms. Arahburu had no
involvement whatsoever in this project while working for the
BCDC, either as a full-time employee or during the period of her
part-time, limited-term contract. 
She has been asked to assist
the City with this project.
11061401


Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
April 2 1- , 199 2
Page 3
We seek confirmation of the telephone advice provided by
,fohn Wa1lace on April 16, L992. Mr. Wallace advised me that,
pursuant to Government Code section 87405, Ms. Aramburu is
prohibited from appearing before or communication with the BCDC
until June 17, 1992, if the appearance or communication is made
for the purpose of influencing any action or proceeding
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a
permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of
goods or property. 
In other words, Ms. Aranburu's part-time,
limited-term contract with the BCDC does not extend the one-
year "cooling off" period required by Government Code section
87 405.
We would also like confirmation of Mr. Wallace's telephone
advice of April L4, L992. On that date, Mr. Wallace advised me
that the remodel plans for the various Pier 39 commercial
facilities 
are "new" proceedings for the purposes of Government
Code sections 87401 and 87402. Accordingly, until June 17,
1992, Ms. Aramburu may assist clients of GCA in connection with
such remodel plans so long as she does not communicate with or
appear before the BCDC. Commencing June L7, L992, Ms. Ara:nburu
also may communicate with or appear before the BCDC on such
remodel- pIans.
1I. 
LEGAL ANAIYSIS.
The so-ca]Ied "revolving door" provisions of the Act (Gov't
Code ss 87400, et seq. ) consist of two parts: the Milton Marks
Postgovernment Employment Restrictions Act of 1990 (Gov't Code s
87406) and the original revolving door provisions (Gov't Code ss
87400-8740s).
A. 
The Milton Marks Postqovernment Employment section
87406 ) .
Government Code section 87405 prohibits a designated
employee of a state administrative agency, for a period of one
year after leaving office or employment, for compensation, from
acting as aqent or attorney for, or otherwise representingr anY
other person, by making any formal or informal appearance. or by
making any oral or written comnunication, before any state
administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, for which
he or she worked or represented during the 12 months before
leaving office of employment, if the appearance or communication
is made for the purpose of influencing administrative action, or
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance,
amendment. awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant,
or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. As
11061401




Scott Hallabrin, Esq,
Apr iL 27, 7992
Page 4
Bay Design Analyst with the BCDC, IIs. Aramburu was a designated
employee of a state administrative agency within the meaning of
Section 87406. In addition, her work for GCA will 1ike1y
involve the issuance, amendment, awarding or revocation of
permits of the BCDC.
As stated previously, we believe Ms. Aramburu is prohibited
from appearing before or corurunicating with the BCDC on permit
matters until June l7 | L992 (one year following her termination
as a designated employee). In other words, we conclude that Ms.
Aramburu's part-time, limited-term employment with BCDC does not
affect her termination status for purposes of Section 87406
because such employment is outside the scope of the statute.
First, aa Mr. Wallace and I discussed in our telephone
conversation of April 16, 1992, IJls. Aramburu was not a
designated employee of the BCDC during her limited-term contract
with that agency, nor did she serve the BCDC in a capacity which
required the exercise of discretion during that period. Second,
it would be contrary to public policy to penalize Ms. Aramburu
for her efforts to assist the state during a crisis, especially
when such assistance involved only ministerial duties. 
Fina11y,
AIan Pendl-eton, Executive Director of the BCDC, has informed us
that it is his belief that Ms. Aramburu's termination from the
Commission was June 1,6, L991 and that the restrictions of
Section 87405 therefore expire after June 16t 1992. See
attached letter to Mr. Pendleton, dated March 1-2, L992t and
acknowledged by him on March L3, L992. Please confirm this
analysis.
The original revolving door provisions were designed to
ensure that state employees working on a governmental matter
which affects a specific party cannot then leave state service
to work for the same party on that same matter. Gorman Advice
Letter, A-80-12-105. Our question is whether the fact that Ms.
Aramburu's work as an employee of BCDC invoLved review of
remodel plans for various tenants of Pier 39 precludes her from
assisting clients of GCA with the approval of new remodel p1ans.
In determining whether the original revolving door
provisions app1y, the following questions arise:
1. 
Is the person in question a former state
administrative of f icial?
Is the matter in which the person now wishes to
be invofved a "proceeding" and one in which he or
B.
11061401
2.




Scott Hallabrin, Eeq.
April 21, 19 92
Page 5
Only if the response is affirmative to
these revolving door provisions apply.
A-9 1-017 .
she participated in when he or she was a state
adminietrative of f icial?
3. 
Is the state a party or does it have a direct and
substantial interest in the proceeding?
4. 
will the personts involvement include
representing another person or aiding, advisj-ng,
counseling, consulting or assisting in
repreeenting any other person in the proceeding?
5. 
will the person be performing those tasks for
compensation ?
aII these
Blanchard
questions wiIl
Advice Letter,
As a former BCDC staff member, Ms. Aramburu qualifies as a
former state administrative official. 
See Gov't Code
s 87400(b). The BCDC would be a party to the proceedings
involving approval of remodel plans, Ms. Ararnburu would be
assisting clients of GCA and would do so for compensation. The
only remaining question is whether the matters on which she
worked as a state employee are the sErme proceedings in which she
would represent clients of GCA.
Section 87400(c) provides that a "proceeding" is "any
proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other
determination, contract, c1ain, controversy, investigation,
charge, accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving
a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative
agency." Many of the matters within BCDC's jurisdiction involve
a specific party who proposes to develop or use a certain piece
of land in a specific manner and thus are "proceedings" for
purposes of the revolving door provisions. The FPPC has advised
that, in certain circumstances, the distinct phases of the
proceedings for development or use of a specific piece of land
are separate proceedings for purposes of the revolving door
provisions, because each phase has a distinct element of
decision maki-ng and different type of review. Chandler Advice
Letter, I-91,-L34i Gorman Advice Letter, supra.
For example, the FPPC has advised that the Coastal
Commission's review of a county's focal coastal plan consists of
three distinct phases. the tand use plan, the approval of zoning
pursuant to the provisions of the general plan, and specific
coastal development permit approval, each of which is considered
a separate Coastal- Conunission proceeding. Berriqan Advice
11061401




Scott Hallabrin, Esq.
ApriI 21, 19 92
Page 5
Letter, A-86-045i see also Gorman Advice Letter, supra.
Moreover, in connection with Coastal Commission permit
applications, the FPPC has stated that "tal new permj-t
application, even if it involves the same tract of 1and, or some
of the sErme issues, as a previous application, ordinarily is
considered a new proceeding. " Anderson Advice Letter, A-86-324
lcitations omittedl. Similarly, once a decision to grant or
deny a coastal permit has been made, subsequent amendments and
other proceedings related to that same coastal permit are
considered new proceedings. Evans Advice letter, l-86-LL7.
As Mr. Wallace and I discussed in our telephone
conversation of April 74, 1992, the FPPC advice most analogous
to the present situation was considered in the Galanter Advice
Letter, A-82-079. There, the South Coast RegioiII-t6EEEEf--
Commission had considered and approved a permit for development
of a large tract of 1and. The tract was divided into areas,
some of which were approved "in concept" only and could not be
developed until a subsequent permit application for a specific
proposed development was submitted and approved.
One of the areas approved "in concept" was sold to a
developer who proposed to hire Ms. Galanter, a former member of
the South Coast Regiona} Coastal Commission, to assist in
obtaining the specific development permit. Even though Ms.
Galanter had participated in the initial 
"in concept" permit
decisions concerning the same area of Iand, the FPPC advised
that she could assist the developer with the subsequent permit
to develop the land. The FPPC concluded that, despite the fact
that the land parcel involved in both permits was the same, the
developer would be commencing a new proceeding because different
issues were invoLved.
The FPPC took a similar approach in the Leslie Advice
Letter, I-90-066. There, Mr. Leslie, a Coastal Commission
employee, administered an affordable housing program involving
several hundred individual properties developed in accordance
with permits approved by the Coastal Commission pr.i-or to L981.
Such permits included restrictions on the sale of the
properties. Mr. Leslie did not work on the initial 
permits. 
He
did review and approve individual amendments to some of the
initial 
permits. The amendments changed the conditions imposed
upon the sale of the properties in question. The FPPC advised
that each individual amendment to the initial 
permits was a
distinct proceeding since each was considered separately, even
though all such amendments were administered under the sarne
Coastal Commission program and involved the same general
policies. 
Therefore, the FPPC advised Mr. Leslie that he could
leave state employment and be involved in an application to
11061401


Scott Eallabrin, Esq.
April 21, 1992
Page 7
antend a specific permit if he had not been involved in the
review and approval of the same arnendment during his tenure with
the Coastal Commiseion.
The6e fact patterns compare favorably to Ms. Aramburu's
work on remodel plans, since BCDC considers each remodel plan
separately for compliance with the initial 
Pier 39 perrnit.
Although the remodel plans involve the s€rme general tract of
land (Pier 39) involved in the work Ms. Aramburu performed while
employed by BCDC, the tenant usually is different and the issues
addressed in a particular remodel plan are substantially
different from those addressed in her work on the Underwater
World amendment proposal or other remodel- plans. As with the
separate permits and separate permit amendments described in the
Galanter and Leslie advice letters, each remodel stands alone
and requires a Beparate and distinct assessment as to whether it
complies with the overall permit for Pier 39.
Based on the FPPC's interpretation of what constitutes a
"new" proceeding as described above, we believe that Ms.
Arambulu's prop5sed work on new remodel plans* constitutes work
on new proceedings which is not precluded by the revolving door
provisions of the Act. 
PLease confirm this analysis.
Should you have any questions, please do not hesitate to
contact me.
Very truly yours,
dm-2."- (-, Ut-,^-**"*
Kathryn E. Donovan
Enc .
cc: Debra Stein, Esq. (w/o enc. )
Ms. Margit Aramburu (w/o enc. )
Mr. F. K. Lowe11 (w/o enc. )
Mr. B. W. Maas (w/o enc. )
* 
Of course, if, during her employment with BCDC, Ms.
Aramburu considered a remodel application which was ultimately
denied on a technicality or was merely continued or resubmitted
in substantially the same form, then the remodel application in
that instance could be considered as the same proceeding. See
Galanter Advice I,e!!er, supra (technicality); Chandler Advice
@tion1.
11061401


Scott IIallabrin, Esq.
April 2L, L9 92
Page 8
bc: Mr. E. w. LeBaron, Jr. (w/o enc. )
Ms. T. C. Lahaderne (w/o enc. )
Ms. S. M. Carey (w/o enc. )
Ms. A. D. S. Mayo (w/o enc. )
11061401
